worth_it_explanation: Out-of-page formats such as interstitials and anchor ads sit outside the normal content flow, so a publisher can build them by hand with floating divs or let Google Publisher Tag (GPT) manage them through defineOutOfPageSlot. The managed path hands placement, frequency, and dismissal behavior to Google, which keeps the format inside the policy rules Google enforces and out of the layout problems a hand-built float invites. The check is a read of the GPT calls and the rendered page, so it costs inspection time rather than a rebuild, and the finding points to a concrete implementation change the developer can make.
intro: Out-of-page formats are the ad units that live outside the page's content column, such as a full-page interstitial, an anchor ad pinned to the top or bottom of the viewport, and a rewarded ad. GPT can manage these formats directly through defineOutOfPageSlot with an OutOfPageFormat value, which lets Google place the unit, control how often it shows, and handle dismissal. The alternative is a hand-built floating div that the publisher positions and shows on their own. This subcategory checks whether the supported out-of-page formats run as GPT-managed slots and whether the out-of-page units are configured so they do not overlap content or shift the layout.

audit_checks.title: Supported out-of-page formats are hand-built rather than GPT-managed slots
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When a supported out-of-page format runs as a hand-built floating div instead of a GPT-managed slot, the publisher takes on placement, frequency capping, and dismissal behavior that Google would otherwise manage to its own policy. That moves the unit outside the guardrails Google enforces for interstitials and anchor ads, which raises the risk of a policy strike and gives up the format-specific handling that the managed slot provides.

educate.base:
Google Publisher Tag (GPT) defines a standard ad slot with defineSlot, which pairs an ad unit path with a div the publisher places in the page. Out-of-page formats work differently. Calling defineOutOfPageSlot with an OutOfPageFormat value tells GPT to manage the unit itself, so Google creates the container, decides where it sits, controls how often it shows, and handles how the user dismisses it. The supported managed formats include the full-page web interstitial, the anchor ad pinned to the top or bottom of the viewport, and the rewarded ad. For an anchor ad and a web interstitial, GPT generates its own container, so the developer does not define a div at all.

The alternative is to build the format by hand. A developer can position a floating div over the content, request a standard ad into it, and write their own logic for when it appears and how it closes. The unit may look the same to a visitor, but it now sits outside the handling Google built for these formats. Google's policy for interstitials and anchor ads covers placement, frequency, and dismissal, and a GPT-managed slot keeps the unit inside those rules. A hand-built float relies on the developer to match that policy exactly, and a mismatch can draw a policy strike against the inventory.

Letting GPT manage the slot also hands off behavior that is awkward to reproduce by hand. The managed interstitial picks its own trigger moments and respects a frequency cap. The managed anchor ad checks that the page supports the format before it requests an ad, and defineOutOfPageSlot returns null when the page cannot support it, which gives the developer a clean signal to skip the request rather than force a unit onto a page that should not carry it.

A few signs point to a supported format being hand-built rather than GPT-managed:
  • A floating or fixed-position div holds an ad that behaves like an interstitial or an anchor, but the code requests it through defineSlot into that div rather than through defineOutOfPageSlot
  • The code contains custom JavaScript that positions, shows, or closes the unit instead of relying on GPT to manage it
  • No defineOutOfPageSlot call with an OutOfPageFormat value appears for a format that GPT supports as a managed slot
You may want to confirm that each supported out-of-page format runs through defineOutOfPageSlot with the matching OutOfPageFormat value. One approach is to read the GPT calls in the page source and the network request to securepubads, then check whether the interstitial and anchor units are managed slots or hand-built floats.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site running GPT, anchor ads and interstitials carry real revenue across long article and section pages, so the temptation to hand-build a floating unit for tighter control is real. You may want to move any hand-built interstitial or anchor to a GPT-managed slot so the placement and frequency stay inside Google's policy and the unit gains the dismissal handling readers expect.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site running GPT, an interstitial that fires between a category page and a product page can interrupt a shopper mid-task if it is hand-built without proper frequency control. You may want to run that interstitial as a GPT-managed slot so Google caps how often it shows and handles dismissal, which keeps the format from getting in the way of the purchase path.

investigate.intro: Review the Google Publisher Tag (GPT) calls in the page source and the rendered behavior to see whether each supported out-of-page format runs as a GPT-managed slot or a hand-built floating unit.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the page and load DevTools, then view the page source and search for 'defineOutOfPageSlot' and 'defineSlot' to list how each ad unit is defined.

For any interstitial or anchor unit, check whether it is requested through defineOutOfPageSlot with an OutOfPageFormat value (e.g., INTERSTITIAL, TOP_ANCHOR, BOTTOM_ANCHOR) or through defineSlot into a floating div.
In the 'Elements' panel, inspect the rendered ad container and note whether GPT generated it or whether the developer placed a fixed-position or floating div by hand.
In the 'Network' panel, filter for 'securepubads' and read the ad request to confirm the out-of-page format is part of the GPT request rather than a separate hand-built call.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open the Google Publisher Console on the page by appending '?googfc' to the URL or running the googfc bookmarklet.
Review the list of defined slots and note which ones are out-of-page formats managed by GPT.
Confirm that each interstitial or anchor unit appears as a GPT-managed slot in the console rather than being absent, which would suggest it is hand-built outside GPT.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each out-of-page format on the site, whether it is requested through defineOutOfPageSlot with the matching OutOfPageFormat value or built as a hand-positioned floating div. A setup where every supported format (interstitial, anchor, rewarded) runs as a GPT-managed slot passes. A setup where a supported format runs as a hand-built float, with custom positioning or dismissal code standing in for GPT's management, is a fail recorded with the format and the code involved.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether each out-of-page format runs through defineOutOfPageSlot or as a hand-built floating div above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs a supported out-of-page format as a hand-built floating unit rather than a Google Publisher Tag (GPT) managed slot. A hand-built float takes on the placement, frequency, and dismissal handling that GPT would otherwise manage to Google's policy for interstitials and anchor ads, which moves the unit outside those guardrails and risks a policy strike. {{voice_recommend}} moving each supported format to defineOutOfPageSlot with the matching OutOfPageFormat value, so Google manages where the unit sits, how often it shows, and how it closes. After the change, recheck the GPT calls and the rendered page to confirm the format runs as a managed slot.

audit_checks.title: Out-of-page units overlap content or cause layout problems
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An out-of-page unit that covers a navigation control, hides content behind a fixed bar, or pushes the page down when it loads degrades the reading experience and can shift the layout. A layout shift from an ad that loads without reserved space worsens Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS), a Core Web Vitals metric, and an overlap that blocks content or a close control can draw a policy concern as well.

educate.base:
Out-of-page formats sit on top of or alongside the content rather than inside the content column, which is what makes them effective and also what makes them easy to get wrong. An anchor ad pinned to the bottom of the viewport can cover a navigation bar or a cookie banner. A full-page interstitial that does not present a clear close control can trap a visitor. A side rail can crowd the content on a narrow window. The format is doing its job when it stays clear of the content and the controls a visitor needs, and it becomes a problem when it overlaps them.

When Google Publisher Tag (GPT) manages an anchor ad or a web interstitial, it generates its own container and places it according to Google's rules for the format, which keeps the unit out of the content flow and supplies the expected dismissal behavior. That handling reduces the overlap risk, but it does not remove the publisher's own responsibility. A fixed header or a sticky element the publisher built can still collide with a managed anchor ad, and a hand-built out-of-page unit carries the full risk on its own.

Layout shift is the second concern. When an ad container loads without space reserved for it, the content around it moves to make room, and that movement counts against CLS, the Core Web Vitals metric that measures unexpected movement of page content. Google's guidance on CLS names third-party ads that resize themselves as a common cause of layout shift. A managed anchor ad generates its own container outside the content flow, which avoids pushing content, but an in-content ad slot placed near an out-of-page unit, or a hand-built float that inserts itself into the layout, can still move the page when it fills.

A few signs point to out-of-page units that overlap content or shift the layout:
  • An anchor ad covers a navigation control, a cookie banner, or a footer link, or it stays visible when the visitor has dismissed it
  • An interstitial appears without a clear close control, or it fires on a page load or an interaction where it gets in the way of the task
  • Content visibly jumps when an out-of-page unit or a nearby ad slot loads, which points to a missing reserved space and a worsened CLS score
You may want to confirm that each out-of-page unit stays clear of content and controls and does not move the page when it loads. One approach is to load the page on mobile and desktop widths, watch for overlap and movement as the units fill, and read the CLS contribution in a performance trace.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site running GPT, a bottom anchor ad on a long article can cover the comment field, a related-links bar, or a sticky share control that readers rely on. You may want to check the anchor ad against the site's own fixed elements on mobile and desktop, and confirm content does not jump when the unit loads, so the format adds revenue without getting in the reader's way.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site running GPT, an out-of-page unit that overlaps an 'Add to cart' button or a product image, or that shifts a product page as it loads, can cost a sale and frustrate a shopper. A layout shift on a commercial page carries a direct cost, so you may want to verify that no anchor or interstitial covers a purchase control and that product and category pages hold steady when the units fill.

learn_more.source_title: Get started with Google Publisher Tag

investigate.intro: Review how each out-of-page unit renders at mobile and desktop widths to see whether it overlaps content or controls, and check whether it moves the page when it loads.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the page in DevTools and use the device toolbar to load it at a narrow mobile width and a desktop width, then trigger each out-of-page unit (e.g., scroll to surface the anchor ad, navigate to fire the interstitial).
Watch whether any unit covers a navigation control, a cookie banner, a footer link, or a purchase control, and whether the anchor ad provides and respects a close control.
Record a trace in the 'Performance' panel while the page loads, then read the layout-shift entries to see whether an out-of-page unit or a nearby ad slot contributes to Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
Note any case where content visibly jumps as a unit fills, which points to space that was not reserved for the slot.

gpt_console.label: Google Publisher Console
steps: Open the Google Publisher Console with '?googfc' or the googfc bookmarklet and locate each out-of-page slot.
Use the console to confirm which out-of-page units rendered and where, then compare that against the visible page to spot any overlap with content or controls.
Note whether a managed anchor or interstitial behaved as expected or whether a publisher-built fixed element collided with it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each out-of-page unit, whether it stays clear of content and controls at mobile and desktop widths and whether it moves the page when it loads. A setup where every out-of-page unit avoids overlap, presents a working close control where the format calls for one, and loads without shifting content passes. A setup where a unit covers a control, blocks content, or contributes to Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) is a fail recorded with the unit, the width, and the problem observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether each out-of-page unit overlaps content or controls and whether it shifts the layout, at mobile and desktop widths, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that an out-of-page unit on {{site_name}} overlaps content or shifts the layout when it loads. An anchor or interstitial that covers a navigation control or a purchase button gets in the visitor's way, and an ad that loads without reserved space moves the surrounding content, which worsens Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS), a Core Web Vitals metric. {{voice_recommend}} testing each out-of-page unit at mobile and desktop widths to confirm it stays clear of content and controls, reserving space for any in-content slot near an out-of-page unit, and relying on Google Publisher Tag (GPT) managed slots so Google places the format outside the content flow. After the change, recheck the rendered page and the CLS contribution to confirm the units no longer overlap or move the layout.
